title: Bronchiolitis
objectives: Recognize clinical features of bronchiolitis in infants
Understand that supportive care is the mainstay of treatment
Apply evidence-based approach avoiding ineffective therapies

sections.title: Definition and Epidemiology
sections.content:
Bronchiolitis is acute inflammation of the small airways (bronchioles) in infants, causing wheezing and respiratory distress.

**Epidemiology:**
• Most common lower respiratory infection in infants <1 year
• Peak incidence: 2-6 months of age
• Peak season: November-March (Northern hemisphere)
• ~3% of infants hospitalized in first year of life
• Leading cause of hospitalization in infants

**Etiology:**
• RSV: 50-80% of cases (most common)
• Rhinovirus: 10-30%
• Human metapneumovirus: 5-10%
• Parainfluenza, influenza, adenovirus, coronavirus
• Dual viral infections common (~30%)

sections.title: Pathophysiology
sections.content:
**Mechanism:**
Viral infection causes:
1. Bronchiolar epithelial cell necrosis
2. Inflammatory infiltrate (lymphocytes, neutrophils)
3. Edema of submucosa and adventitia
4. Increased mucus production
5. Bronchiolar obstruction → air trapping and atelectasis

**Why Infants Are Vulnerable:**
• Small airway diameter (resistance ∝ 1/r⁴)
• Immature immune response
• Highly compliant chest wall (less effective recoil)
• Obligate nasal breathers (nasal congestion = major problem)
• Decreased smooth muscle → less bronchodilator response

**Clinical Consequence:**
Ball-valve obstruction creates hyperinflation in some areas and atelectasis in others, causing V/Q mismatch and hypoxemia.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Typical Course:**
• URI prodrome (rhinorrhea, cough, low-grade fever) for 2-4 days
• Progressive respiratory distress over 3-5 days
• Peak severity: Days 3-5
• Resolution over 1-2 weeks (cough may persist longer)

**Physical Exam:**
• Tachypnea (often >60/min)
• Nasal flaring, grunting
• Subcostal, intercostal, suprasternal retractions
• Expiratory wheezing and crackles
• Prolonged expiratory phase
• Hyperinflated chest
• Poor feeding, dehydration

**Apnea:**
• May be presenting sign in young infants (<2 months)
• Can occur without significant respiratory distress
• More common in former preterm infants

sections.title: High-Risk Features
sections.content:
**Risk Factors for Severe Disease:**

**Patient Factors:**
• Age <3 months (especially <6 weeks)
• Prematurity (<32 weeks gestation)
• Chronic lung disease (BPD)
• Congenital heart disease (especially cyanotic or with pulmonary hypertension)
• Immunodeficiency
• Neuromuscular disease

**Clinical Features Suggesting Severity:**
• Respiratory rate >70/min
• SpO2 <90% on room air
• Nasal flaring, grunting, severe retractions
• Apnea
• Inability to feed
• Altered mental status/lethargy
• Cyanosis

**Predictors of Need for ICU:**
• Hypoxemia despite supplemental O2
• Apnea
• Respiratory fatigue/failure

sections.title: Diagnosis
sections.content:
**Bronchiolitis is a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS**

**Routine Testing NOT Recommended:**
• Chest X-ray (unless ruling out other diagnoses)
• Viral testing (rarely changes management)
• Blood tests (CBC, CMP not helpful)
• Blood cultures (unless concern for bacterial infection)

**When to Consider Testing:**
• Chest X-ray: Suspected bacterial pneumonia, foreign body, cardiac disease
• Viral PCR: Infection control (cohorting), epidemiology, immunocompromised patients
• Blood culture: Febrile infant <60 days, toxic appearance

**CXR Findings (if obtained):**
• Hyperinflation
• Peribronchial thickening
• Atelectasis (commonly right upper lobe)
• May have patchy infiltrates (viral pneumonitis)

sections.title: Treatment: What Works
sections.content:
**SUPPORTIVE CARE IS THE MAINSTAY**

**Oxygen:**
• Supplemental O2 if SpO2 <90% (some guidelines use <92%)
• Target SpO2 ≥90%
• Continuous pulse oximetry while on oxygen

**Hydration:**
• Assess feeding ability and hydration status
• NG feeds or IV fluids if unable to feed orally
• Small, frequent feeds may be better tolerated

**Nasal Suctioning:**
• Bulb suction or nasal aspirator PRN
• Most effective before feeds and sleep
• Avoid deep/frequent suctioning (causes edema)

**High-Flow Nasal Cannula (HFNC):**
• For moderate-severe cases
• Provides positive pressure, humidification, washout of dead space
• May prevent intubation

sections.title: Treatment: What Does NOT Work
sections.content:
**The Following Are NOT RECOMMENDED:**

**Bronchodilators (Albuterol):**
• NO benefit in bronchiolitis
• May cause tachycardia without improvement
• AAP recommends against routine use
• Consider trial ONLY if clear asthma history

**Corticosteroids:**
• NO benefit in bronchiolitis
• Do not reduce hospitalization or improve outcomes
• Not recommended (unlike in asthma/croup)

**Nebulized Hypertonic Saline:**
• Conflicting evidence
• May have modest benefit in hospitalized patients
• NOT recommended in ED setting

**Antibiotics:**
• Bronchiolitis is viral
• Reserve for documented bacterial coinfection

**Chest Physiotherapy:**
• No benefit, may worsen distress

sections.title: Disposition and Prevention
sections.content:
**Admission Criteria:**
• Hypoxemia (SpO2 <90% on room air)
• Moderate-severe respiratory distress
• Apnea
• Poor oral intake/dehydration
• Age <2-3 months
• High-risk comorbidities
• Unreliable follow-up/social concerns

**Discharge Criteria:**
• Stable or improving respiratory status
• SpO2 ≥90% on room air
• Adequate oral intake
• Reliable caregivers with return precautions

**Prevention:**

**Palivizumab (Synagis):**
• RSV monoclonal antibody (monthly IM injection)
• For high-risk infants: preterm, CHD, CLD

**Nirsevimab (Beyfortus):**
• Long-acting RSV antibody (single dose)
• Approved for ALL infants <8 months during RSV season
• Given before or during first RSV season

**General Measures:**
• Hand hygiene
• Avoid sick contacts
• Avoid tobacco smoke exposure

keyTakeaways: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in infants; routine testing is not recommended
RSV is the most common cause; peak age is 2-6 months
Supportive care only: O2, hydration, nasal suctioning
Bronchodilators and steroids do NOT work (unlike asthma)
High-risk: age <3 months, prematurity, CHD, chronic lung disease
Nirsevimab (Beyfortus) now available for RSV prevention in all infants

references.citation: Ralston SL, Lieberthal AS, Meissner HC, et al. Clinical Practice Guideline: The Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of Bronchiolitis. Pediatrics. 2014;134(5):e1474-e1502
references.keyContent: AAP evidence-based guideline - supportive care mainstay, bronchodilators not recommended

references.citation: Friedman JN, Rieder MJ, Walton JM. Bronchiolitis: Recommendations for diagnosis, monitoring and management of children one to 24 months of age. Paediatr Child Health. 2014;19(9):485-498
Meissner HC. Viral Bronchiolitis in Children. N Engl J Med. 2016;374(1):62-72
references.keyContent: Comprehensive NEJM review of pathophysiology and management

references.citation: Hammitt LL, Dagan R, Yuan Y, et al. Nirsevimab for Prevention of RSV in Healthy Late-Preterm and Term Infants. N Engl J Med. 2022;386(9):837-846
references.keyContent: Landmark trial supporting nirsevimab (Beyfortus) for RSV prevention

reviewQuestions.question: A 4-month-old with RSV bronchiolitis is wheezing and has SpO2 of 88% on room air. After suctioning and supplemental oxygen, SpO2 improves to 94%. What additional treatment is most likely to improve outcomes?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Supportive care (oxygen, hydration, suctioning) is the mainstay of bronchiolitis treatment. Bronchodilators and steroids have not shown benefit. Hypertonic saline evidence is weak.

reviewQuestions.question: A 6-week-old former 34-week preterm infant presents with bronchiolitis. What feature makes this infant high-risk?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Young age (<3 months) and prematurity are both independent risk factors for severe bronchiolitis. This infant has both, placing them at high risk for complications including apnea.

reviewQuestions.question: Parents ask about preventing RSV in their healthy 2-month-old. What is the current recommendation?
options: No prevention available for healthy infants
reviewQuestions.explanation: Nirsevimab (Beyfortus) is now recommended for all infants <8 months during their first RSV season. It's a single dose, unlike palivizumab which required monthly injections and was only for high-risk infants.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected bronchiolitis?

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in bronchiolitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination

coachDx.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Obtain a thorough history and physical examination from Consult a specialist before any workup.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of bronchiolitis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically

coachDx.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Consider the differential diagnosis systematically from Order every available test to rule out all possibilities.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.

mcqs.stem: When managing bronchiolitis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values

coachDx.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values from Always defer to the most recent published study.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.

mcqs.stem: A newborn pulse oximetry screen shows 88% on the right hand and 95% on the left foot. What does this suggest?

options.text: Reverse differential cyanosis — suggests transposition of the great arteries with coarctation
options.explanation: Normally, right hand (preductal) SpO2 should be HIGHER than foot (postductal). Here it's reversed: foot > right hand. This 'reverse differential cyanosis' occurs when oxygenated blood from a PDA preferentially supplies the descending aorta while deoxygenated blood goes to the upper body — classic for TGA with coarctation or interrupted aortic arch.

options.text: Normal variant — up to 10% difference between extremities is acceptable
options.explanation: A >3% difference is abnormal on CCHD screening. Moreover, the right hand being LOWER than the foot is never normal and suggests specific cardiac pathology.

options.text: Equipment malfunction — repeat testing on the same extremity
options.explanation: While equipment error should be considered, a specific pattern (reverse differential) with both readings in clinically relevant ranges warrants urgent cardiac evaluation, not dismissal.

options.text: Standard differential cyanosis from a large PDA with left-to-right shunt
options.explanation: Standard differential cyanosis (lower SpO2 in feet than right hand) occurs with right-to-left PDA shunting. Here the pattern is REVERSED (right hand lower), which has a different differential.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Reverse differential cyanosis — suggests transposition of the great arteries with coarctation.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Reverse differential cyanosis — suggests transposition of the great arteries with coarctation

coachDx.misconception: While equipment error should be considered, a specific pattern (reverse differential) with both readings in clinically relevant ranges warrants urgent cardiac evaluation, not dismissal.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Reverse differential cyanosis — suggests transposition of the great arteries wit from Equipment malfunction — repeat testing on the same extremity.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Reverse differential cyanosis — suggests transposition of the great arteries with coarctation.
